question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isEnabled()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a Docker environment variable override (`-e HIERO_MIRROR_IMPORTER_DOWNLOADER_RECORD_ENABLED=false`) in a shared container orchestration environment to disable record downloading, causing the mirror node to stop tracking Hashgraph history and allowing an attacker to later re-enable it pointing to a tampered data source?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getCommon()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate `hiero.mirror.importer.downloader.bucketName` via environment variable injection to point the record downloader to an attacker-controlled S3-compatible bucket containing forged record files with valid-looking but tampered Hashgraph history, which the importer then ingests as legitimate?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getCommon()] Can an unprivileged external user set `hiero.mirror.importer.downloader.endpointOverride` to an attacker-controlled S3-compatible endpoint via environment variable injection, causing `RecordDownloaderProperties.getCommon()` to resolve downloads from a malicious source that serves tampered record files, corrupting the Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isWriteFiles()] Can an unprivileged external user enable `writeFiles=true` and then exploit a symlink in the archive destination directory to redirect written record files to a location readable by other processes, leaking verified Hashgraph record content and enabling an attacker to craft a tampered replacement before the next verification cycle?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getFrequency()] Can an unprivileged external user inject an extremely large `frequency` value (e.g., `PT87600H`) via a Spring Boot property source, causing the `fixedDelayString` SpEL expression `#{@recordDownloaderProperties.getFrequency().toMillis()}` to produce a delay of years, effectively freezing the Hashgraph history at the current state and preventing any new legitimate records from being ingested?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: setEnabled(boolean)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a race condition between `setEnabled(false)` and the `@Scheduled` download task to cause the downloader to process exactly one batch of tampered record files before disabling itself, inserting forged transactions into the Hashgraph history while appearing to have been briefly enabled by a transient configuration error?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getCommon()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `allowAnonymousAccess` flag in `CommonDownloaderProperties` (accessible via `getCommon()`) to enable anonymous access to a bucket, then replace legitimate record files in that bucket with tampered ones, causing the record downloader to ingest forged Hashgraph history without credential-based access controls blocking the substitution?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isPersistBytes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `persistBytes=false` default to ensure that after a tampered record file is ingested and parsed, the raw bytes evidence is discarded, making it impossible for a forensic investigator to detect the tampering by comparing stored bytes against the original Hashgraph record files?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getStreamType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a deserialization or bean injection vulnerability to replace the `StreamType.RECORD` returned by `getStreamType()` with `StreamType.BLOCK`, causing the record downloader to apply block-stream verification logic (which has different hash-chain rules) to record files, bypassing record-specific integrity checks and allowing tampered Hashgraph history to be accepted?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isEnabled()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a Spring Boot `@RefreshScope` misconfiguration to trigger a context refresh that resets `enabled` to `true` after an operator has intentionally disabled it for maintenance, causing the downloader to resume ingesting from a tampered upstream source during the maintenance window?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getCommon()] Can an unprivileged external user set `hiero.mirror.importer.downloader.maxSize=1` via environment variable injection, causing `getCommon().getMaxSize()` to return 1 byte, which causes all legitimate record files (which are larger) to be rejected as oversized, effectively halting Hashgraph history ingestion while an attacker prepares a tampered minimal-size record file that fits within the limit?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getCommon()] Can an unprivileged external user set `hiero.mirror.importer.downloader.batchSize=0` via environment variable injection, causing `getCommon().getBatchSize()` to return zero, which prevents any signature files from being downloaded per batch, stalling the record verification pipeline and creating a window where tampered files can be staged in the upstream bucket?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: setWriteSignatures(boolean)] Can an unprivileged external user enable `writeSignatures=true` and then exploit a file permission misconfiguration on the archive directory to overwrite written signature files with forged ones, so that any future re-verification of the Hashgraph history using archived signatures would validate the tampered record files?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getFrequency()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the SpEL expression `#{@recordDownloaderProperties.getFrequency().toMillis()}` in the `@Scheduled` annotation by injecting a `frequency` value that causes `toMillis()` to overflow to a negative number (e.g., `PT9223372036854775807S`), causing the scheduler to interpret it as an immediate or invalid delay and disrupting the orderly sequential processing of Hashgraph record files?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isEnabled()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a Helm chart misconfiguration that exposes the `hiero.mirror.importer.downloader.record.enabled` property as a public Kubernetes annotation, allowing the attacker to read the current state and craft a timing attack that disables the downloader precisely when a tampered record file is available in the upstream bucket?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getCommon()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `pathPrefix` property in `CommonDownloaderProperties` (via `getCommon()`) by injecting a path traversal string (e.g., `../../tampered/`) to redirect the record downloader to fetch files from an attacker-controlled path within the same bucket, serving tampered Hashgraph record files instead of legitimate ones?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getCommon()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `pathType` property in `CommonDownloaderProperties` (via `getCommon()`) by switching it from `ACCOUNT_ID` to `NODE_ID` or `AUTO` via environment variable injection, causing the record downloader to look for record files in a different bucket path structure where an attacker has pre-staged tampered Hashgraph record files?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isWriteFiles()] Can an unprivileged external user enable `writeFiles=true` and exploit a TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) race condition between when a record file is verified and when it is written to the archive path, replacing the file on disk with a tampered version after verification but before archival, so the archived copy contains forged Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getCommon()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `downloadRatio` property in `CommonDownloaderProperties` (via `getCommon()`) by setting it to a value lower than `consensusRatio` via environment variable injection, triggering the `IllegalArgumentException` in `validateRatios()` that crashes the importer on startup, preventing any Hashgraph record files from being ingested and creating a denial-of-history scenario?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: isEnabled()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a Spring Boot Actuator `/actuator/refresh` endpoint (if unauthenticated) to force a configuration refresh that re-reads environment variables, and if the attacker has previously injected `HIERO_MIRROR_IMPORTER_DOWNLOADER_RECORD_ENABLED=false` into the process environment, this refresh would disable record downloading and freeze the Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getFrequency()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a minimum duration constraint on `frequency` (only `@NotNull` is enforced, no `@DurationMin`) to set `frequency=PT0.000000001S` (1 nanosecond), causing the scheduler to fire millions of times per second, exhausting CPU and preventing the hash-chain verification from completing, effectively corrupting the sequential integrity of Hashgraph history ingestion?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: setPersistBytes(boolean)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a Spring Boot property binding vulnerability to set `persistBytes` to a non-boolean truthy string that gets coerced to `true`, causing raw record bytes to be persisted to the database where they can be exfiltrated and analyzed to craft a more convincing tampered Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/record/RecordDownloaderProperties.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getCommon()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `timeout` property in `CommonDownloaderProperties` (via `getCommon()`) by setting it to `PT0.001S` (1 millisecond) via environment variable injection, causing all record file download operations to time out before completion, preventing legitimate Hashgraph record files from being ingested while an attacker stages tampered files in the bucket?
